title: Bounds and wildcards

sources.claim: Bounds and wildcards express which types an API accepts: extends supports safe production, super supports safe consumption, and a named type parameter relates multiple positions.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Bounds and wildcards express which types an API accepts: extends supports safe production, super supports safe consumption, and a named type parameter relates multiple positions.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A sum method accepts List<? extends Number> because it reads Numbers without inserting values into the caller's collection. Your goal is to choose extends, super or a type parameter for a collection-processing API and explain why. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Interfaces; Arrays and multidimensional arrays; Generic types and methods; Class inheritance. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: Bounds and wildcards express which types an API accepts: extends supports safe production, super supports safe consumption, and a named type parameter relates multiple positions. This model is applied through the declared Java: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: Generic types are invariant, wildcard capture limits writing, null remains writable to many producer views, and PECS is guidance rather than a substitute for the exact contract. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Because Integer extends Number, List<Integer> is automatically a subtype of List<Number>.

includes: Express producer, consumer and subtype constraints without assuming invariant generic types are covariant.
Choose extends, super or a type parameter for a collection-processing API and explain why.
excludes: Generic types are invariant, wildcard capture limits writing, null remains writable to many producer views, and PECS is guidance rather than a substitute for the exact contract.

outcomes: Choose extends, super or a type parameter for a collection-processing API and explain why.

examples.title: Bounds and wildcards: direct language case
examples.situation: A sum method accepts List<? extends Number> because it reads Numbers without inserting values into the caller's collection.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Java: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A sum method accepts List<? extends Number> because it reads Numbers without inserting values into the caller's collection.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Java generics are invariant; use ? extends for a producer, ? super for a consumer, or a named type parameter when positions must share one type.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Java generics are invariant; use ? extends for a producer, ? super for a consumer, or a named type parameter when positions must share one type.

examples.title: Bounds and wildcards: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A copy method writes Integer values into List<? super Integer> and uses a named T only when source and destination element relationships must be shared.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A copy method writes Integer values into List<? super Integer> and uses a named T only when source and destination element relationships must be shared.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: Generic types are invariant, wildcard capture limits writing, null remains writable to many producer views, and PECS is guidance rather than a substitute for the exact contract.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Because Integer extends Number, List<Integer> is automatically a subtype of List<Number>.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Choose extends, super or a type parameter for a collection-processing API and explain why.

recap.summary: You should now be able to choose extends, super or a type parameter for a collection-processing API and explain why. Keep this boundary in view: Generic types are invariant, wildcard capture limits writing, null remains writable to many producer views, and PECS is guidance rather than a substitute for the exact contract.
